title: Thermoelectric enhancement from an asymmetric spectral-conductivity cusp in spin-1 chiral fermions
authors: Risako Kikuchi, Junya Endo, Ai Yamakage
abstract: A recent study showed that, in spin-1 chiral fermion systems composed of two linearly dispersing bands and one trivial band, impurity scattering produces an asymmetric cusp in the spectral conductivity. We demonstrate that this asymmetric cusp markedly enhances the electronic thermoelectric response. Using linear-response theory within the self-consistent Born approximation, we find low-temperature enhancements in both the Seebeck coefficient and the electronic figure of merit. Increasing the curvature of the trivial band further strengthens this cusp-induced enhancement, even though the corresponding density of states becomes smoother. To clarify this mechanism, we introduce a minimal cusp model for the spectral conductivity and show that the enhancement is most pronounced when the cusp is sharp and strongly asymmetric, and when the spectral conductivity at the cusp energy is small.
summary: This paper shows that the asymmetric features in conductivity caused by impurities can actually be leveraged to boost thermoelectric efficiency in spin-1 chiral fermion systems. By analyzing the interplay between band curvature and scattering, the authors identify specific regimes where the Seebeck coefficient and figure of merit are significantly enhanced.
main_problem: The study investigates how impurity scattering, which creates an asymmetric cusp in spectral conductivity, enhances the electronic thermoelectric response in spin-1 chiral fermion systems.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the asymmetric cusp markedly enhances the Seebeck coefficient, power factor, and electronic figure of merit, with the effect being strongest when the cusp is sharp, asymmetric, and has low background conductivity.
method: The researchers use linear-response theory within the self-consistent Born approximation (SCBA) and employ a minimal cusp model to isolate the underlying transport mechanism.
model_or_system: The system consists of spin-1 chiral fermions in topological semimetals, modeled by a Hamiltonian with two linearly dispersing bands and one nearly flat trivial band, subject to Gaussian finite-range impurity scattering.
key_observables: Density of states (DOS), spectral conductivity, Seebeck coefficient, power factor, electronic figure of merit (ZT_el), and Lorenz number.
important_parameters: Scattering strength (W), dimensionless band curvature (c_tilde), reduced temperature (T_tilde), and cusp asymmetry/sharpness parameters (gamma, p, q).
main_assumptions: The study focuses only on electronic contributions to thermal conductivity and assumes a low-temperature regime where impurity scattering dominates over phonon contributions.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the band structure, conductivity cusp, and ZT enhancement; Fig 2 displays temperature dependence of transport coefficients; Figs 8-10 present color maps of the Seebeck coefficient and Lorenz ratio in the (p, q) parameter plane.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the spin-1 chiral fermion model and the impurity-induced cusp, presents numerical results using SCBA, transitions to a generalized minimal cusp model to clarify the mechanism, and concludes with an analytical discussion of the parameter space for thermoelectric optimization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) Schematic overview of the physical picture and the main result of this work. (a) Band structure of an effective model for a threefold-degenerate spin-1 chiral fermion system. (b) Asymmetric cusp in the spectral con- ductivity at ϵc (red dashed line) [52]. (c) Electronic figure of merit ZT (el), showing an enhancement near ϵc found in the present work.

FIG. 2. (Color online) Temperature dependences of thermoelectric response at fixed W = 2 and ˜c = 0.1. (a) Density of states D(ϵ) versus ϵ. (b) Spectral conductivity σ(ϵ) versus ϵ. (c) Seebeck coeﬀicient S versus chemical potential µ. (d) Lorenz ratio L/L0 versus µ. (e) Power factor PF versus µ. (f) Electronic figure of merit ZT (el) versus µ. Panels (c)–(f) share a common color code for temperature: ˜T = 0.005 (green), ˜T = 0.0075 (orange), and ˜T = 0.010 (indigo). In panels (b)–(f), the red dotted horizontal line marks ϵ = ϵc in (b) and µ = ϵc in (c)–(f).

Figure 5 shows the dependence on the curvature pa- rameter ˜c of the trivial band. The results are obtained with the parameters fixed at W = 2 and ˜T = 0.005. As the curvature increases, the density of states becomes smoother as a function of energy [Fig. 5(b)], while the asymmetry of the spectral-conductivity cusp becomes more pronounced [Fig. 5(c)]. Below ϵc, the spectral con- ductivity decreases more gradually, whereas above ϵc, it increases more steeply as a function of energy. Corre- spondingly, the peaks in the Seebeck coeﬀicient and fig- ure of merit increase with curvature [Figs. 5(d) and 5(e)].
FIG. 3. (Color online) Electronic figure of merit ZT (el) as a function of ˜T for the spin-1 chiral fermion model with W = 2 and ˜c = 0.1, evaluated at the cusp chemical potential µ = ϵc.

FIG. 4. (Color online) Scattering-strength W dependence of thermoelectric response at fixed ˜c = 0.1 and ˜T = 0.005. (a) Color map of the spectral conductivity σ(ϵ) in the (W, ϵ) plane. (b) σ(ϵ) versus ϵ. (c) Seebeck coeﬀicient S versus chemical potential µ. (d) Figure of merit ZT (el) versus µ. Panels (b)–(d) share a common color code: W = 2 (purple), W = 3 (blue), W = 5 (green).
